description: Full English phoneme catalog for the Cadence coaching engine. Each entry defines how to teach, describe, and coach a phoneme. Covers General American (GA) as primary target with RP notes where relevant. https://soundsamerican.net/article/vowel_sound_tense_i_as_in_be

æ.label: Short A
æ.articulation: Low front vowel. Open your mouth wide, spread your lips slightly, and keep the tongue low and pushed forward.
æ.learner_tip: Think of the sound a surprised person makes — short, open, forward. Longer than /ɛ/, wider than /ɑ/.
common_errors: Raised too high, sounding like /ɛ/ (especially before nasals)
Replaced with /ɑː/ by Spanish or Portuguese speakers
Pin-pen merger: sounds like /ɪ/ before nasals in Southern American English

ɛ.label: Short E
ɛ.articulation: Mid front vowel. Mouth half-open, lips slightly spread, tongue mid-height and forward.
ɛ.learner_tip: Relax your jaw halfway between /iː/ and /æ/. Think of 'eh?' — a questioning sound.
common_errors: Confused with /æ/ — /ɛ/ is higher and less open
Confused with /eɪ/ — keep it short and pure, no glide
Replaced with /e/ (tenser version) by European speakers

ɪ.label: Short I
ɪ.articulation: High front lax vowel. Tongue near the top of the mouth but relaxed, slightly lower than /iː/. Lips spread but loose.
ɪ.learner_tip: Say /iː/ then relax and lower your tongue slightly. The sound is shorter and less tense.
common_errors: Replaced with /iː/ — keep it short and lax, not tense
Confused with /ɛ/ by French or Spanish speakers
Merged with /iː/ in some dialects (e.g. feel/fill)

ɒ.label: Short O (RP)
ɒ.articulation: Open back rounded vowel (RP). Mouth open, tongue low and back, lips loosely rounded.
ɒ.learner_tip: In GA this vowel merges with /ɑː/ (the lot-palm merger). In RP it is short and rounded. Think of a British 'pot'.
common_errors: GA speakers may not distinguish this from /ɑː/
Over-rounded by learners aiming for RP
ɒ.dialect_note: In General American, /ɒ/ and /ɑː/ are merged. This phoneme is primarily an RP distinction.

ʌ.label: Short U
ʌ.articulation: Open-mid central to back vowel. Mouth half-open, tongue central, no lip rounding.
ʌ.learner_tip: Relax everything. Neutral, unstressed sound in the middle of the mouth. Like a short grunt.
common_errors: Confused with /ʊ/ — /ʌ/ has no lip rounding
Replaced with /ɑː/ or /a/ by European speakers
Not distinguished from /ʊ/ in some dialects (foot-strut merger)

ʊ.label: Short OO
ʊ.articulation: High back lax rounded vowel. Tongue high and back, lips loosely rounded. Less tense than /uː/.
ʊ.learner_tip: Like /uː/ but shorter and with less lip rounding. Say 'oo' then relax your lips and tongue.
common_errors: Replaced with /uː/ — keep it short and lax
Confused with /ʌ/ — /ʊ/ has lip rounding
Not distinguished from /uː/ in some dialects (pool-pull merger)

ə.label: Schwa
ə.articulation: Mid central neutral vowel. Fully relaxed mouth, tongue in the center, no lip rounding. The most common English sound.
ə.learner_tip: The schwa only appears in unstressed syllables. Never stress a schwa — it should be quick and throwaway.
common_errors: Over-pronouncing it as a full vowel (common in speakers whose L1 lacks schwa reduction)
Spelling-based pronunciation: saying 'sup-PORT' with a clear /o/ instead of schwa
Missing it in consonant clusters

iː.label: Long E
iː.articulation: Close front tense vowel. Tongue high and forward, lips spread into a smile position. Hold it longer than /ɪ/.
iː.learner_tip: Spread your lips like a big smile and hold the sound. Your tongue should feel near the roof of your mouth.
common_errors: Shortened to /ɪ/ — hold the length and tension
Not distinguished from /ɪ/ in some dialects

uː.label: Long OO
uː.articulation: Close back tense rounded vowel. Tongue high and back, lips strongly rounded and protruded.
uː.learner_tip: Round your lips into a tight circle as if kissing. The sound is long and tense.
common_errors: Shortened to /ʊ/ — hold the length and rounding
Under-rounded by speakers whose L1 lacks rounded vowels

ɑː.label: Long AH
ɑː.articulation: Open back unrounded vowel. Mouth wide open, tongue low and back, no lip rounding. Like saying 'ah' at the doctor.
ɑː.learner_tip: Drop your jaw completely and say 'ah'. Keep it long and open, no movement of tongue or lips.
common_errors: Shortened by non-rhotic speakers adding /r/ where none exists
Confused with /æ/ — /ɑː/ is further back and longer

ɔː.label: AW Sound
ɔː.articulation: Open-mid back rounded vowel. Mouth open, tongue mid-back, lips loosely rounded.
ɔː.learner_tip: Round your lips loosely and push the tongue back slightly. Similar to /ɒ/ but longer and more rounded.
common_errors: Merged with /ɑː/ in GA (cot-caught merger) — many American speakers don't distinguish these
Under-rounded by non-native speakers
ɔː.dialect_note: In many American dialects, /ɔː/ and /ɑː/ have merged (cot-caught merger).

ɜː.label: ER Sound
ɜː.articulation: Open-mid central unrounded vowel. Tongue in mid-central position. In GA it is r-colored (retroflex).
ɜː.learner_tip: In American English, curl the tip of your tongue back slightly while making this sound — it's an r-colored vowel.
common_errors: Missing the r-color in GA — it should sound like 'er' not 'uh'
Adding too much r-color in RP, where /ɜː/ is non-rhotic
Confused with /ʌ/ — /ɜː/ is longer and often r-colored
ɜː.dialect_note: In RP (British English) the final r is not pronounced — 'bird' sounds like /bɜːd/. In GA it is rhotic — /bɜːrd/.

eɪ.label: Long A
eɪ.articulation: Starting position: mid front /e/. Glide upward to /ɪ/. The tongue rises and the jaw closes slightly.
eɪ.learner_tip: Start with a relaxed /e/ sound then let your tongue glide up. Feel the two positions — don't stop in the middle.
common_errors: Shortened to a pure /e/ without the glide (common in Spanish/Italian speakers)
Over-gliding so it sounds like 'ay-ee'

aɪ.label: Long I
aɪ.articulation: Starting position: open /a/ (or /ɑ/). Glide up to /ɪ/. Jaw closes, tongue rises.
aɪ.learner_tip: Start with an open mouth /ah/, then close and raise your tongue toward /ee/. It's a big movement.
common_errors: Starting too high — begin with a genuinely open mouth
Monophthongization: reduced to just /aː/ in some Southern American dialects

ɔɪ.label: OY Sound
ɔɪ.articulation: Starting position: /ɔː/ (back rounded). Glide forward and up to /ɪ/. Lips start rounded then spread.
ɔɪ.learner_tip: Start with rounded lips like /ɔː/, then glide your tongue forward and up. Feel the rounding release.
common_errors: Under-rounding the start position
Not completing the glide to /ɪ/

aʊ.label: OW Sound
aʊ.articulation: Starting position: open /a/. Glide back and up to /ʊ/. Lips start spread and become rounded.
aʊ.learner_tip: Begin open and spread, then round your lips as the tongue moves back and up. Feel the lip movement.
common_errors: Starting too far back like /ɔ/
Not completing the glide — the oo ending is important

oʊ.label: Long O
oʊ.articulation: Starting position: mid back /o/. Glide up and back to /ʊ/. Lips begin slightly rounded and increase.
oʊ.learner_tip: Begin with rounded lips and let them tighten as the sound glides. Think 'oh-oo' in one smooth motion.
common_errors: Realized as pure /oː/ without the glide (many European speakers)
Starting position too open like /ɑ/

ɪə.label: EAR Sound
ɪə.articulation: Starting position: /ɪ/ (high front). Glide to /ə/ (center). Tongue relaxes and moves back.
ɪə.learner_tip: Start with /ɪ/ and let your tongue relax down and back to schwa. It should flow naturally.
common_errors: GA speakers often produce this as /ɪr/ (rhotic): 'near' = /nɪr/
Over-separating into two distinct vowels
ɪə.dialect_note: In GA this is typically /ɪr/ (rhotic). /ɪə/ is an RP distinction.

eə.label: AIR Sound
eə.articulation: Starting position: /e/ (mid front). Glide to /ə/. Tongue relaxes toward center.
eə.learner_tip: Begin at /e/ then let everything relax. The movement is subtle — don't over-exaggerate.
common_errors: GA speakers produce this as /ɛr/ — 'care' = /kɛr/
Confused with /eɪ/ by forgetting the schwa glide
eə.dialect_note: In GA this merges with /ɛr/ (rhotic). /eə/ is primarily an RP distinction.

θ.label: TH (Voiceless)
θ.articulation: Voiceless dental fricative. Place the tip of your tongue lightly against or between your upper and lower teeth. Push air through — do NOT vibrate your vocal cords.
θ.learner_tip: Stick your tongue tip slightly between your teeth and blow air. Feel that it is silent — no buzz from your throat.
common_errors: Replaced with /s/ (common in Japanese, Spanish, Asian language speakers): 'think' → 'sink'
Replaced with /t/ (common in many L1s): 'think' → 'tink'
Voiced incorrectly as /ð/: 'thin' → 'then'

ð.label: TH (Voiced)
spelling_patterns: th (function words and some content words)
ð.articulation: Voiced dental fricative. Same tongue position as /θ/ but add vocal cord vibration — you should feel a buzz.
ð.learner_tip: Same as /θ/ but hum while you do it. Place your fingers on your throat — you should feel vibration.
common_errors: Replaced with /d/ (very common): 'the' → 'de'
Replaced with /z/ (common in European speakers): 'this' → 'zis'
Devoiced to /θ/: 'breathe' → 'breeth'

ʃ.label: SH Sound
ʃ.articulation: Voiceless postalveolar fricative. Tongue behind the alveolar ridge (not touching), lips slightly rounded. Air rushes through a wider opening than /s/.
ʃ.learner_tip: Like /s/ but pull your tongue back slightly and round your lips. It's a hushing sound — 'shh!'
common_errors: Confused with /s/ — /ʃ/ is further back and has lip rounding
Confused with /tʃ/ — /ʃ/ is continuous, no stop

ʒ.label: ZH Sound
ʒ.articulation: Voiced postalveolar fricative. Same as /ʃ/ but with vocal cord vibration.
ʒ.learner_tip: Say /ʃ/ then add voice from your throat. Feel the buzz. It sounds like the middle of 'measure'.
common_errors: Devoiced to /ʃ/
Replaced with /dʒ/ by adding a stop at the start

tʃ.label: CH Sound
tʃ.articulation: Voiceless postalveolar affricate. Start with the tongue against the ridge like /t/, then release into /ʃ/. It is one sound, not two.
tʃ.learner_tip: Press your tongue to the ridge, build pressure, then release into a hush. 'ch' in 'cheese' is one quick motion.
common_errors: Replaced with /ʃ/ alone (missing the stop onset)
Replaced with /ts/ (common in Japanese speakers)

dʒ.label: J Sound
dʒ.articulation: Voiced postalveolar affricate. Same as /tʃ/ but with vocal cord vibration throughout.
dʒ.learner_tip: Like /tʃ/ but add voice. Feel the buzz as you release. 'Jump' starts with the tongue on the ridge.
common_errors: Devoiced to /tʃ/
Replaced with /y/ by some speakers (common in German L1): 'jump' → 'yump'

ŋ.label: NG Sound
ŋ.articulation: Velar nasal. Tongue back presses against the soft palate (velum). Air flows through the nose. No lip or tongue-tip movement.
ŋ.learner_tip: Say 'ng' like the end of 'sing' — feel the back of your tongue raised and touching the roof. No /g/ at the end in most GA words.
common_errors: Adding a /g/ at the end: 'sing' → 'sing-g' (hypercorrection)
Replacing with /n/: 'sing' → 'sin'
Nasalizing the preceding vowel instead of producing a proper nasal stop

ɹ.label: R Sound
ɹ.articulation: Voiced alveolar approximant (GA). Curl the tongue tip back (retroflex) or bunch the tongue body up without touching the palate. Lips may be slightly rounded.
ɹ.learner_tip: Never let your tongue touch anything for /ɹ/. Curl the tip back or pull the body up — it floats. This is the most uniquely American sound.
common_errors: Replaced with a tap /ɾ/ (flap) by Spanish speakers: 'red' → 'ɾed'
Replaced with a trill /r/ by European speakers
Replaced with /l/ by some East Asian speakers: 'rice' → 'lice'
Over-rhotic: too much curl causing a 'pirate' sound

l.label: L Sound
l.articulation: Voiced alveolar lateral approximant. Tongue tip touches the alveolar ridge (behind upper teeth). Air flows around the sides. In syllable-final position, it is 'dark' /ɫ/ — the tongue body raises at the back.
l.learner_tip: Touch the ridge behind your upper teeth with your tongue tip. The tip stays up. For 'dark L' (final position), push the tongue body back as well.
common_errors: Replaced with /r/ (especially by East Asian speakers): 'light' → 'right'
Missing the 'dark L' in final position: 'full' → 'fuh'
Vocalized dark L: 'full' → 'fouw'

v.label: V Sound
v.articulation: Voiced labiodental fricative. Upper teeth lightly touch the lower lip. Air flows through creating friction, with vocal cord vibration.
v.learner_tip: Rest your upper teeth on your lower lip and push air through while humming. Feel the lip vibrating.
common_errors: Replaced with /w/ (common in Japanese and some South Asian speakers): 'very' → 'wery'
Replaced with /b/ (common in Spanish speakers): 'van' → 'ban'
Devoiced to /f/: 'live' → 'life'

w.label: W Sound
w.articulation: Voiced labial-velar approximant. Lips are tightly rounded and protruded, tongue is raised toward the velum. Then open into the following vowel.
w.learner_tip: Start with very rounded lips like /uː/, then quickly open into the next vowel. It is a glide — no friction.
common_errors: Confused with /v/: not a fricative — no teeth involved
Under-rounded so it sounds like a plain vowel
